name: Pasta all'Amatriciana
desc: Roman pasta with guanciale, tomato, pecorino, and chili flakes
steps: Crisp guanciale in olive oil until golden, remove and set aside.
Add tomatoes and chili flakes to the rendered fat, simmer 15 minutes.
Cook bucatini until al dente, toss with sauce and guanciale.
Serve topped with grated pecorino romano.
tips: Use guanciale, not pancetta, for authentic flavor.
Pecorino romano is traditional; Parmesan changes the dish.

name: Pasta Puttanesca
desc: Bold Neapolitan pasta with olives, capers, anchovies, and tomatoes
steps: Saute garlic and anchovies in olive oil until anchovies dissolve.
Add tomatoes, olives, capers, and chili flakes; simmer 15 minutes.
Cook spaghetti al dente and toss with sauce.
Serve immediately with fresh parsley.
tips: Anchovies melt into the sauce and add umami, not fishiness.
Do not add cheese — it's not traditional for this dish.

name: Cacio e Pepe
desc: Classic Roman pasta with pecorino cheese and black pepper
steps: Toast black pepper in a dry pan until fragrant.
Cook pasta in salted water, reserving 2 cups pasta water.
Combine finely grated pecorino with a ladle of pasta water to form a cream.
Toss hot pasta with pecorino cream and pepper, adding pasta water as needed.
tips: Add pasta water gradually to avoid clumping the cheese.
Use freshly cracked pepper for the best flavor.

name: Pasta alla Norma
desc: Sicilian pasta with fried eggplant, tomato sauce, and ricotta salata
steps: Slice and salt eggplant, let drain 20 minutes, then fry until golden.
Saute garlic in olive oil, add tomatoes and basil, simmer 15 minutes.
Cook rigatoni al dente and toss with tomato sauce.
Top with fried eggplant and crumbled ricotta salata.
tips: Salting the eggplant removes bitterness and excess moisture.
Ricotta salata can be substituted with feta in a pinch.

name: Fettuccine Alfredo
desc: Creamy Roman-style fettuccine with butter and Parmigiano-Reggiano
steps: Cook fettuccine in well-salted water until al dente.
Melt butter in a wide pan, add a ladle of pasta water.
Toss hot fettuccine with butter, adding Parmigiano gradually.
Serve immediately with fresh cracked pepper.
tips: The original Roman version uses only butter and cheese, no cream.
Toss vigorously to emulsify the cheese into a silky sauce.

name: Pasta e Fagioli
desc: Hearty Italian soup with small pasta and cannellini beans
steps: Saute onion, celery, and garlic in olive oil until soft.
Add tomatoes, beans, and broth; simmer 20 minutes.
Mash half the beans for a thicker texture, add pasta.
Cook until pasta is tender, serve with olive oil drizzle.
tips: Use small pasta shapes that fit on a spoon.
This soup thickens as it sits; add broth when reheating.

name: Ribollita
desc: Tuscan bread soup with cannellini beans, kale, and vegetables
steps: Saute onion, carrot, and celery in olive oil until soft.
Add tomatoes, beans, and kale; cook until kale wilts.
Tear bread into pieces and stir into soup; simmer 20 minutes.
Let rest 10 minutes before serving drizzled with olive oil.
tips: Use day-old Tuscan bread for the best texture.
Ribollita means 'reboiled' — it's even better the next day.

name: Osso Buco alla Milanese
desc: Braised veal shanks in white wine with gremolata
steps: Season and flour veal shanks, brown in olive oil on all sides.
Saute onion, carrot, and celery; add white wine and reduce by half.
Add broth, cover, and braise in oven at 160C for 2 hours.
Serve topped with gremolata of lemon zest, garlic, and parsley.
tips: Don't skip the gremolata — it brightens the rich braise.
Traditionally served with risotto alla milanese (saffron risotto).

name: Saltimbocca alla Romana
desc: Veal escalopes wrapped with prosciutto and sage in white wine sauce
steps: Place sage leaf and prosciutto on each veal piece, secure with toothpick.
Dust lightly with flour, pan-fry prosciutto-side down in butter 2 minutes.
Flip and cook 2 more minutes, remove from pan.
Deglaze pan with white wine, reduce to a sauce and pour over meat.
tips: Saltimbocca means 'jumps in the mouth' in Italian.
Pound the veal thin for quick, even cooking.

name: Risotto alla Milanese
desc: Golden saffron risotto from Milan with Parmigiano and butter
steps: Saute onion in butter, add rice and toast 2 minutes.
Add white wine and stir until absorbed.
Ladle in warm broth gradually, stirring, with saffron dissolved in first ladle.
Finish with cold butter and Parmigiano, rest 2 minutes before serving.
tips: Use real saffron threads, not powder — toast them first for more flavor.
The final butter stir (mantecatura) creates the creamy texture.

name: Risotto ai Frutti di Mare
desc: Seafood risotto with shrimp, mussels, and calamari
steps: Saute garlic in olive oil, add seafood and cook 3 minutes; remove.
Toast rice in the same pan, add wine and reduce.
Add fish stock gradually, stirring, with halved cherry tomatoes.
Return seafood in final 2 minutes, finish with parsley.
tips: Never add cheese to seafood risotto — it's an Italian rule.
Use a mix of shrimp, mussels, and calamari rings.

name: Eggplant Parmigiana
desc: Baked layers of fried eggplant with tomato sauce and mozzarella
steps: Slice and salt eggplant, fry in olive oil until golden.
Make tomato sauce with garlic, canned tomatoes, and basil.
Layer eggplant, sauce, mozzarella, and Parmigiano in a baking dish.
Bake at 180C for 30 minutes until bubbly and golden.
tips: Salting eggplant draws out moisture for crispier frying.
Let it rest 10 minutes after baking for cleaner slices.

name: Tiramisu
desc: Classic Italian dessert with espresso-soaked ladyfingers and mascarpone cream
steps: Separate eggs; whisk yolks with sugar until pale and thick.
Fold mascarpone into yolk mixture until smooth.
Whip egg whites to stiff peaks and fold into mascarpone mixture.
Dip ladyfingers in espresso, layer with cream, refrigerate 6 hours, dust with cocoa.
tips: Strong, cooled espresso works best — not instant coffee.
Refrigerate at least 6 hours for the best set texture.

name: Panna Cotta
desc: Silky Piedmontese cream dessert with vanilla and berry coulis
steps: Bloom gelatin in cold water for 5 minutes.
Heat cream with sugar and vanilla until just simmering; stir in gelatin.
Pour into ramekins and refrigerate at least 4 hours.
Make berry coulis by simmering berries with lemon juice, serve alongside.
tips: Do not boil the cream or the gelatin will lose its setting power.
The wobble test: it should jiggle like jelly when set.

name: Cannoli
desc: Sicilian fried pastry tubes filled with sweet ricotta cream
steps: Make dough with flour, Marsala, lard, and cinnamon; rest 30 minutes.
Roll thin, wrap around cannoli tubes, fry at 180C until golden.
Drain ricotta, mix with powdered sugar, chocolate chips, and cinnamon.
Fill shells just before serving to keep them crisp.
tips: Fill cannoli right before eating — they go soggy quickly.
Drain ricotta overnight in cheesecloth for the smoothest filling.

name: Pizza Quattro Formaggi
desc: Four-cheese pizza with mozzarella, gorgonzola, fontina, and Parmigiano
steps: Stretch dough into a round on a floured surface.
Tear mozzarella and distribute with crumbled gorgonzola and cubed fontina.
Grate Parmigiano over top and drizzle with olive oil.
Bake at 250C for 8-10 minutes until crust is charred and cheese is bubbling.
tips: Preheat your oven as hot as it goes with a pizza stone inside.
Add a drizzle of honey after baking for a sweet-savory twist.

name: Pizza Diavola
desc: Spicy Italian pizza with tomato, mozzarella, and spicy salami
steps: Stretch dough into a round and spread tomato sauce evenly.
Top with torn mozzarella and sliced spicy salami.
Sprinkle chili flakes and drizzle olive oil.
Bake at 250C for 8-10 minutes until bubbly and charred.
tips: Use nduja (spicy spreadable salami) for extra heat.
Let the pizza rest 2 minutes before slicing.

name: Focaccia
desc: Ligurian olive oil flatbread with rosemary and flaky sea salt
steps: Mix flour, yeast, water, and 2 tbsp oil; knead until smooth.
Let rise 1 hour, then stretch into an oiled sheet pan.
Dimple with fingers, drizzle remaining oil, top with rosemary and salt.
Bake at 220C for 20 minutes until golden and crispy.
tips: Generous olive oil in the pan creates the crispy bottom.
Let dough rest 10 minutes if it springs back when stretching.

name: Arancini
desc: Sicilian fried risotto balls stuffed with mozzarella and peas
steps: Mix cold risotto with one beaten egg and cooked peas.
Shape into balls with a cube of mozzarella in the center.
Dip in beaten egg, coat in breadcrumbs.
Deep fry at 180C for 3-4 minutes until golden and crisp.
tips: Use day-old risotto — it's firmer and easier to shape.
Freeze for 30 minutes before frying for a better hold.

name: Vitello Tonnato
desc: Piedmontese cold sliced veal with creamy tuna-caper sauce
steps: Poach veal in salted water with aromatics for 40 minutes, let cool.
Blend tuna, mayonnaise, capers, anchovies, and lemon juice until smooth.
Slice veal thinly and arrange on a platter.
Drape tuna sauce over veal, garnish with capers and lemon.
tips: Chill the veal completely before slicing for clean cuts.
This dish is best made a day ahead and refrigerated.

name: Carpaccio di Manzo
desc: Paper-thin raw beef slices with arugula, Parmigiano, and lemon dressing
steps: Freeze beef for 30 minutes, then slice paper-thin with a sharp knife.
Arrange slices on a cold plate, covering the surface.
Top with arugula, shaved Parmigiano, and capers.
Drizzle with lemon juice and olive oil, season with salt and pepper.
tips: Semi-frozen beef is much easier to slice thin.
Use the highest quality beef you can find.

name: Gnocchi al Pomodoro
desc: Pillowy potato gnocchi in fresh tomato basil sauce
steps: Boil potatoes whole, peel, rice while hot, mix with flour and egg.
Roll into ropes, cut into pieces, press with a fork for ridges.
Make sauce: saute garlic, add tomatoes and basil, simmer 15 minutes.
Boil gnocchi until they float, toss with sauce and serve.
tips: Use starchy potatoes and work the dough as little as possible.
Over-mixing makes gnocchi tough — stop as soon as dough comes together.

name: Gnocchi al Gorgonzola
desc: Potato gnocchi in a creamy gorgonzola and walnut sauce
steps: Melt butter with gorgonzola and cream over low heat, stirring.
Toast walnuts in a dry pan and roughly chop.
Boil gnocchi until they float, drain and add to sauce.
Toss gnocchi in sauce, top with walnuts and pepper.
tips: Use dolce gorgonzola for a milder, creamier sauce.
Don't boil the sauce or the cheese will separate.

name: Penne alla Vodka
desc: Penne in a creamy tomato-vodka sauce with Parmigiano
steps: Saute garlic and chili flakes in olive oil.
Add tomatoes, simmer 10 minutes, then add vodka and cook 3 minutes.
Stir in cream and cook until sauce thickens slightly.
Toss with cooked penne and Parmigiano.
tips: The vodka enhances tomato flavor — the alcohol cooks off.
Add pasta water to adjust sauce consistency.

name: Orecchiette con Cime di Rapa
desc: Puglian ear-shaped pasta with broccoli rabe, garlic, and anchovies
steps: Blanch broccoli rabe in boiling salted water 2 minutes, remove with a slotted spoon.
Cook orecchiette in the same water until al dente.
Saute garlic, anchovies, and chili in olive oil; add broccoli rabe.
Toss with drained pasta and a splash of pasta water.
tips: Cook pasta and broccoli rabe in the same water for better flavor.
Anchovies dissolve into the oil and add savory depth.

name: Insalata Tricolore
desc: Classic Italian salad with radicchio, arugula, and endive
steps: Wash and tear radicchio, separate endive leaves, toss with arugula.
Whisk olive oil and balsamic vinegar with salt.
Drizzle dressing over salad and toss gently.
Serve immediately as a side or starter.
tips: The three colors (red, white, green) represent the Italian flag.
Add shaved Parmigiano for a richer version.

name: Chicken Cacciatore
desc: Hunter-style braised chicken with tomatoes, olives, and herbs
steps: Brown chicken thighs in olive oil, remove and set aside.
Saute onion, pepper, and garlic; deglaze with white wine.
Add tomatoes and olives, return chicken, cover and simmer 40 minutes.
Serve with crusty bread or polenta.
tips: Bone-in, skin-on thighs give the best flavor and texture.
This improves overnight as flavors meld.

name: Fritto Misto
desc: Mixed fried seafood and vegetables with lemon
steps: Clean seafood, slice zucchini into thin rounds.
Make light batter with flour and ice-cold sparkling water.
Deep fry seafood and vegetables in batches at 180C until golden.
Drain on paper towels, season with salt, serve with lemon wedges.
tips: Keep batter ice cold for maximum crispiness.
Don't overcrowd the fryer — fry in small batches.

name: Caponata
desc: Sicilian sweet and sour eggplant relish with tomatoes and capers
steps: Cube and fry eggplant in olive oil until golden, drain on paper towels.
Saute celery until soft, add tomatoes, olives, and capers.
Stir in vinegar and sugar, simmer 10 minutes.
Add fried eggplant, stir gently, cool to room temperature before serving.
tips: Caponata tastes best at room temperature or slightly cool.
Make a day ahead — the flavors develop overnight.

name: Polenta with Mushroom Ragu
desc: Creamy polenta topped with rich mushroom and tomato ragu
steps: Bring broth to a boil, whisk in polenta gradually, stir for 30 minutes.
Saute onion and garlic, add sliced mushrooms, cook until browned.
Add tomatoes to mushrooms and simmer 15 minutes.
Stir butter and Parmigiano into polenta, serve topped with ragu.
tips: Constant stirring prevents lumps in polenta.
Use a mix of porcini, cremini, and shiitake for depth.

name: Cotoletta alla Milanese
desc: Bone-in veal cutlet breaded and fried in butter, Milanese style
steps: Pound veal cutlets to even thickness, keeping the bone.
Dredge in flour, dip in beaten eggs, coat in breadcrumbs.
Fry in clarified butter over medium heat, 4 minutes per side.
Serve immediately with lemon wedges and arugula.
tips: Use clarified butter — whole butter will burn at frying temperatures.
Press breadcrumbs firmly for an even, crispy coating.

name: Panzanella
desc: Tuscan bread salad with tomatoes, cucumber, red onion, and basil
steps: Tear bread into chunks and soak briefly in water, squeeze dry.
Chop tomatoes, cucumber, and onion.
Toss bread with vegetables, basil, oil, and vinegar.
Let sit 15 minutes before serving for flavors to meld.
tips: Use overripe, juicy summer tomatoes for the best result.
Stale bread is essential — fresh bread will turn to mush.

name: Linguine alle Vongole
desc: Classic Neapolitan pasta with clams in white wine and garlic
steps: Saute garlic and chili in olive oil, add clams and white wine, cover.
Cook 5-7 minutes until all clams open; discard any that don't.
Cook linguine al dente, toss with clam sauce and fresh parsley.
Serve immediately with the clams in their shells.
tips: Soak clams in salted water 30 minutes to purge sand.
Never add cheese to clam pasta.

name: Italian Wedding Soup
desc: Light broth with mini meatballs, escarole, and tiny pasta
steps: Mix ground pork with egg, breadcrumbs, Parmigiano, and garlic; form tiny meatballs.
Bring chicken broth to a simmer, add meatballs and cook 10 minutes.
Add chopped escarole and pasta, cook until pasta is tender.
Serve with extra Parmigiano and crusty bread.
tips: Make meatballs marble-sized for the best proportion with the broth.
The 'wedding' refers to the marriage of meat and greens, not an actual wedding.

name: Tortellini in Brodo
desc: Bolognese stuffed pasta served in clear rich meat broth
steps: Make filling by finely chopping prosciutto and mortadella, mixing with Parmigiano, egg, and nutmeg.
Roll pasta thin, cut into squares, place filling and fold into tortellini shape.
Bring rich beef broth to a gentle simmer.
Cook tortellini in broth for 3-4 minutes, serve in bowls with broth.
tips: The traditional tortellini shape mimics Venus's navel, according to legend.
A rich, homemade broth is what makes this dish special.

name: Involtini di Melanzane
desc: Eggplant rolls stuffed with ricotta and baked in tomato sauce
steps: Slice eggplant lengthwise, grill or bake until pliable.
Mix ricotta with Parmigiano and basil, spread on eggplant slices.
Roll up and place seam-side down in a tomato sauce-lined baking dish.
Top with mozzarella and bake at 180C for 25 minutes.
tips: Grilling the eggplant slices adds a smoky flavor.
Don't overfill the rolls or they'll burst open.

name: Spaghetti alle Cozze
desc: Spaghetti with mussels in a white wine and garlic sauce
steps: Saute garlic in olive oil, add mussels and white wine, cover.
Cook until mussels open (5-7 minutes), discard any closed ones.
Add halved cherry tomatoes to the pan, cook 2 minutes.
Toss with cooked spaghetti and fresh parsley.
tips: Scrub and debeard mussels before cooking.
Reserve some mussels in shells for a beautiful presentation.

name: Supplì al Telefono
desc: Roman fried rice croquettes with a stretchy mozzarella center
steps: Take spoonfuls of cold tomato risotto and shape around a mozzarella cube.
Dip in beaten egg and coat in breadcrumbs.
Deep fry at 180C for 3 minutes until golden brown.
Break open to see the mozzarella stretch like a telephone cord.
tips: The name 'al telefono' comes from the stretching mozzarella resembling a phone cord.
Use day-old risotto for the easiest shaping.

name: Pesce all'Acqua Pazza
desc: Neapolitan fish fillets poached in crazy water with tomatoes and olives
steps: Saute garlic in olive oil, add halved cherry tomatoes and cook 3 minutes.
Add white wine, olives, capers, and a cup of water; bring to a simmer.
Nestle fish fillets in the liquid, cover and poach 10 minutes.
Serve fish with the broth spooned over, with crusty bread for dipping.
tips: Acqua pazza means 'crazy water' — named for the lively bubbling broth.
Sea bass, branzino, or red snapper work best.

name: Spaghetti alla Gricia
desc: The original Roman pasta with guanciale and pecorino, no tomato
steps: Render guanciale in a cold pan over medium heat until crisp.
Cook spaghetti al dente, reserving pasta water.
Remove pan from heat, add pasta and toss with guanciale fat.
Add pecorino gradually with pasta water, tossing to create a creamy sauce.
tips: Gricia is the ancestor of both carbonara and amatriciana.
Remove pan from heat before adding cheese to prevent clumping.

name: Cioppino
desc: Italian-American seafood stew with tomatoes, wine, and mixed shellfish
steps: Saute onion, fennel, and garlic in olive oil until soft.
Add white wine and reduce by half, then add tomatoes and simmer 15 minutes.
Add fish first, then shellfish, cover and cook 8-10 minutes.
Serve in deep bowls with grilled bread.
tips: Add shellfish in order of cooking time — fish first, then shrimp, then clams/mussels.
This stew originated in San Francisco's Italian fishing community.

name: Bruschetta al Pomodoro e Basilico
desc: Toasted bread topped with fresh diced tomatoes, garlic, and basil
steps: Dice tomatoes, mix with torn basil, olive oil, and salt; let sit 10 minutes.
Toast or grill bread slices until golden and crisp.
Rub each slice with a cut garlic clove.
Spoon tomato mixture over bread and serve immediately.
tips: Use the ripest, most flavorful tomatoes you can find.
Serve immediately so the bread stays crispy.

name: Zabaglione
desc: Warm Italian egg custard whipped with Marsala wine and sugar
steps: Whisk egg yolks and sugar in a heatproof bowl over simmering water.
Gradually add Marsala, whisking constantly for 8-10 minutes.
Continue until mixture is thick, foamy, and tripled in volume.
Spoon into glasses over fresh berries and serve warm.
tips: Keep whisking constantly or the eggs will scramble.
The bowl should never touch the water — only steam heats it.

name: Pappa al Pomodoro
desc: Tuscan tomato and bread soup, thick and comforting
steps: Saute garlic in olive oil, add chopped tomatoes and cook 15 minutes.
Tear bread into pieces, add to pot with broth and basil.
Simmer 15 minutes, stirring until bread breaks down into a thick porridge.
Serve drizzled with olive oil and fresh basil.
tips: The soup should be thick enough that a spoon stands up in it.
Use summer tomatoes for the best flavor, or San Marzano canned.

name: Chicken Piccata
desc: Pan-fried chicken cutlets in a bright lemon-caper butter sauce
steps: Pound chicken thin, season and dredge in flour.
Pan-fry in olive oil until golden, 3 minutes per side; remove.
Add white wine and lemon juice to pan, scraping up fond.
Swirl in butter and capers, return chicken to pan and spoon sauce over.
tips: Pounding the chicken ensures even, quick cooking.
Add a handful of parsley at the end for color.

name: Affogato
desc: Vanilla gelato drowned in a shot of hot espresso
steps: Place a scoop of gelato in each serving glass.
Pull a fresh shot of hot espresso.
Pour espresso directly over gelato and serve immediately.
Optionally grate dark chocolate on top.
tips: Serve immediately — the magic is in the contrast of hot and cold.
Use high-quality gelato and freshly pulled espresso.

name: Baked Ziti
desc: Italian-American baked pasta with ricotta, mozzarella, and meat sauce
steps: Brown ground beef with garlic, add tomatoes and basil, simmer 20 minutes.
Cook ziti until slightly underdone, mix with ricotta.
Layer pasta, meat sauce, and mozzarella in a baking dish.
Top with Parmigiano and bake at 190C for 25 minutes until bubbly.
tips: Undercook the pasta slightly — it finishes in the oven.
Let rest 10 minutes after baking for neater slices.

name: Parmigiana di Zucchine
desc: Baked layers of zucchini with tomato sauce and mozzarella
steps: Slice zucchini lengthwise, grill or pan-fry until lightly golden.
Make tomato sauce with garlic, canned tomatoes, and basil.
Layer zucchini, sauce, mozzarella, and Parmigiano in a baking dish.
Bake at 180C for 25 minutes until golden and bubbling.
tips: Grilling the zucchini instead of frying makes this lighter.
Salt the zucchini first to remove excess water.

name: Pasta alla Norcina
desc: Umbrian pasta with sausage, cream, and black truffle
steps: Remove sausage from casings and brown in a pan, breaking into crumbles.
Deglaze with white wine and cook until evaporated.
Add cream and simmer until slightly thickened.
Toss with cooked rigatoni and pecorino, finish with black pepper.
tips: Use fennel-spiced Italian sausage for authentic Umbrian flavor.
A few drops of truffle oil at the end elevates this dish.

name: Insalata di Mare
desc: Cold Italian seafood salad with lemon, parsley, and olive oil
steps: Poach seafood separately until just cooked, drain and cool.
Slice celery thinly and mince garlic.
Toss cooled seafood with celery, garlic, parsley, lemon juice, and olive oil.
Chill for at least 1 hour before serving.
tips: Don't overcook the seafood — it should be tender, not rubbery.
Best served at cool room temperature, not ice cold.

name: Ravioli Burro e Salvia
desc: Ricotta and spinach ravioli in brown butter and sage sauce
steps: Wilt spinach, squeeze dry, and mix with ricotta and Parmigiano for filling.
Roll pasta thin, fill and seal ravioli.
Boil ravioli until they float, about 3-4 minutes.
Brown butter with sage leaves and pour over ravioli.
tips: Squeeze every drop of moisture from the spinach.
Browned butter has a nutty aroma — don't let it burn.

name: Farinata di Ceci
desc: Ligurian crispy chickpea flour flatbread baked in olive oil
steps: Whisk chickpea flour with water, salt, and 2 tbsp olive oil; rest 30 minutes.
Pour remaining olive oil into a hot cast iron pan.
Pour in batter, top with rosemary.
Bake at 230C for 15-20 minutes until golden and crispy at edges.
tips: Let the batter rest — this makes it lighter and crispier.
The top should be golden and the edges should be dark and crispy.

name: Stracciatella alla Romana
desc: Roman egg drop soup with Parmigiano in clear chicken broth
steps: Bring chicken broth to a gentle simmer.
Beat eggs with Parmigiano, semolina, parsley, and nutmeg.
Pour egg mixture in a thin stream into simmering broth while stirring.
Cook 2 minutes until egg forms 'little rags' in the broth.
tips: Don't boil vigorously or the egg will break up too finely.
Stracciatella means 'little rags' — the egg shreds in the soup.

name: Peperonata
desc: Slow-cooked sweet peppers with tomatoes and onions
steps: Slice peppers and onion into strips.
Saute onion and garlic in olive oil until soft.
Add peppers and tomatoes, cover and cook on low 30 minutes.
Stir in basil and serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: Use a mix of red, yellow, and orange peppers for color.
Low and slow cooking makes the peppers silky and sweet.

name: Pollo alla Diavola
desc: Spatchcocked chicken grilled with chili, lemon, and herbs
steps: Spatchcock chicken by removing the backbone and pressing flat.
Marinate in lemon juice, olive oil, garlic, chili, and rosemary for 1 hour.
Grill or roast at 220C for 40-45 minutes until skin is crispy.
Rest 10 minutes before carving, serve with pan juices.
tips: Pressing with a heavy pan while grilling ensures even crisping.
The chicken is done when thigh juices run clear (75C internal).

name: Pasta al Limone
desc: Light and creamy lemon pasta from the Amalfi Coast
steps: Cook spaghetti al dente, reserve pasta water.
Melt butter with lemon zest and cream, simmer 2 minutes.
Toss pasta with sauce, adding lemon juice and Parmigiano.
Add pasta water as needed, finish with fresh basil.
tips: Use the zest of all lemons but juice of only two — adjust to taste.
This dish should taste bright, not heavy despite the cream.

name: Porchetta
desc: Slow-roasted pork belly rolled with fennel, garlic, and herbs
steps: Score pork belly skin, rub flesh with fennel, garlic, herbs, and lemon zest.
Roll tightly and tie with kitchen twine, rub skin with salt and oil.
Roast at 160C for 2.5 hours, then increase to 230C for 20 minutes for crackling.
Rest 20 minutes before slicing into thick rounds.
tips: Dry the skin thoroughly before roasting for the crispiest crackling.
Let the rolled porchetta rest overnight in the fridge for best flavor.

name: Braciole
desc: Thin beef rolls stuffed with breadcrumbs and cheese, braised in tomato sauce
steps: Pound beef thin, spread filling of breadcrumbs, Parmigiano, garlic, and parsley.
Roll up tightly and secure with toothpicks or kitchen twine.
Brown rolls in olive oil, deglaze with red wine.
Add tomatoes, cover and braise on low for 2 hours until fork-tender.
tips: The braising sauce becomes a rich pasta sauce — serve it over rigatoni.
Low and slow is the key to tender braciole.

name: Zuppa Toscana
desc: Tuscan soup with Italian sausage, potatoes, and kale in creamy broth
steps: Brown sausage in a pot, breaking into crumbles, remove.
Saute onion and garlic, add sliced potatoes and broth.
Simmer until potatoes are tender, add kale and sausage.
Stir in cream, cook 5 minutes more, serve hot.
tips: Use spicy Italian sausage for extra kick.
The kale should be just wilted, not overcooked.

name: Limoncello Semifreddo
desc: Frozen Italian dessert with limoncello, cream, and meringue
steps: Whip cream to soft peaks, fold in limoncello and lemon zest.
Whisk egg whites with sugar to stiff, glossy peaks.
Gently fold meringue into cream mixture.
Pour into a loaf pan, freeze at least 6 hours, slice to serve.
tips: Line the pan with plastic wrap for easy unmolding.
Remove from freezer 5 minutes before slicing for cleaner cuts.

name: Melanzane alla Griglia
desc: Grilled eggplant with garlic, mint, and vinegar dressing
steps: Slice eggplant into 1cm rounds, brush with olive oil.
Grill on high heat 3-4 minutes per side until charred.
Layer grilled slices with sliced garlic and mint leaves.
Drizzle with vinegar and olive oil, marinate 30 minutes before serving.
tips: This antipasto improves after a few hours of marinating.
Works equally well on a grill pan or outdoor grill.

name: Bucatini alla Carbonara
desc: Thick hollow pasta with egg, guanciale, and pecorino in the Roman style
steps: Render guanciale until crispy, reserve the fat.
Whisk egg yolks with pecorino and generous black pepper.
Cook bucatini al dente, add to guanciale pan off heat.
Pour egg mixture over pasta, toss quickly with pasta water to create silky sauce.
tips: Take the pan off heat before adding eggs to prevent scrambling.
The residual heat and pasta water create the creamy sauce.
